Name: compressnet
Title: Operational Bounds for Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing neural network compression as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive operational bounds on the bits-per-weight needed to preserve predictive sufficiency, and design compression algorithms that achieve these bounds.
Related Work: Previous work on neural network compression has focused on empirical methods such as quantization and pruning. In contrast, our approach is grounded in information-theoretic principles, and seeks to establish fundamental limits on compression rates. Recent advances in rate-distortion theory and the information bottleneck method provide a foundation for our work.
Abstract: This proposal explores the application of rate-distortion theory to neural network compression. We seek to establish operational bounds on the bits-per-weight required to preserve predictive performance, and to design compression algorithms that achieve these bounds. Our approach is grounded in information-theoretic principles, and seeks to provide a fundamental understanding of the trade-offs between compression rate, distortion, and predictive performance.
Experiments: Derive operational bounds on compression rates using rate-distortion theory
Design and evaluate compression algorithms that achieve these bounds
Evaluate the trade-offs between compression rate, distortion, and predictive performance on benchmark datasets
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of neural networks may limit the applicability of rate-distortion theory
The lack of a clear distortion measure for neural networks may hinder the development of effective compression algorithms
The computational resources required to derive and evaluate operational bounds may be significant
